usageRule: This file records clear course-term matches to the CFA Program glossary. It identifies the CFA glossary term and gives a short course-aligned meaning note; use sourceUrl to view the original CFA wording.

matches.meaningFocus: Resources controlled by an enterprise that are expected to bring future economic benefit.
A regulated venue where investment instruments can be traded.
The ability to trade quickly and easily near fair value, or to meet short-term cash needs.
The buy-side price quoted by a dealer or trader for an asset.
The sell-side price quoted by a dealer or trader for an asset.
Common equity shares that rank below other equity claims such as preferred equity.
Amounts from ordinary business activity over a stated period, before deducting expenses.
Sales is generally treated as revenue from goods, while revenue can include goods or services.
Inventory-related cost of goods sold for a period, used as the closest accounting match.
Sales remaining after deducting cost of sales.
Gross profit expressed relative to revenue.
Profit from usual business activities before tax, also called operating income.
Operating income or operating profit divided by revenue.
Net cash provided by operating activities.
Spending on fixed or physical capital.
Cash available after the investments needed to keep the company operating.
A point of reference or comparison for judging performance.
A long-term pattern of movement in one direction.
Return earned by an investor over a specified holding period.
A distribution to shareholders based on the shares they own.
Annual dividend per share compared with the share price.
Price change plus income received over the period.
Income earned during a period for each common share.
Share price divided by earnings per share.
Estimating asset value using future-return variables or comparisons with similar assets.
Idiosyncratic risk tied to one company's operations, reputation or business environment.
A group of related industries.
Uncertainty about how activity will be regulated or how regulation may change.
Risk that laws or regulations change in ways that harm company operations or profitability.
Hybrid investment products with fund-like holdings and exchange-traded features.
Business performance with high sensitivity to business-cycle or industry-cycle influences.
Bank net interest income compared with average earning assets.
Risk that a borrower does not repay principal and interest.
A company with similar business risk, usually in the same industry.
Companies whose sales and profits expand and contract with the business cycle.
Use of fixed costs in operations.
Present obligations expected to require future outflows of economic resources.
Use of fixed costs in a company's operating or financing structure.
Companies whose sales and profits have low sensitivity to the business cycle.
